personality_voice.book_baseline: Macnair is established across multiple books as cold, cruel, and eager for violence. His single line of dialogue in PoA — 'Where is the beast?' — is delivered in a 'cold voice', signalling menace and impatience. He is described as a 'maniac' by Hagrid who notes he 'likes killing as much as Golgomath'. In GoF, Voldemort addresses him with the promise of 'better victims', and Macnair responds with fawning gratitude ('Thank you, Master... thank you'), revealing a servile, bloodthirsty personality. In OotP he is active and dangerous in the DoM battle. His personality is consistent: brutal, obedient to power, and defined by a love of killing.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film corpus is entirely empty. Macnair has no dialogue, no described actions, and no presence in any provided screenplay scene.
personality_voice.difference: Every observable personality trait — the cold voice, the eagerness, the servility toward Voldemort, the mania for killing — is absent from the films as represented in this corpus.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: With zero film material, no personality or voice can be assessed as preserved. The score must reflect near-total absence of the character's established traits.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Macnair serves several distinct narrative functions: (1) in PoA, he is the executioner whose presence creates urgency and whose momentary distraction (being called to sign the paperwork) is the precise window Harry uses to free Buckbeak — he is a direct plot mechanism; (2) in GoF, he is named at the graveyard as a returning Death Eater, establishing Voldemort's reformed inner circle; (3) in OotP, he is an active combatant in the DoM, assigned by Lucius to search with Avery, and is the Death Eater Neville knocks out, directly enabling Harry's escape; (4) in HBP/DH context, he recruits giants for Voldemort and fights in the Battle of Hogwarts where Hagrid throws him into a wall. His agency is real and plot-consequential across three books.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: No film scenes are present in the corpus. His narrative functions — the execution scene tension, the DoM combat, the giant recruitment — cannot be assessed as preserved.
narrative_role_agency.difference: All agency and narrative function is absent from the film corpus. His role as plot mechanism in PoA (the distraction that enables Buckbeak's rescue) and as active combatant in OotP are unrepresented.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The absence of any film material means his narrative role and agency score must be near zero. Even if he appeared briefly in the films, the corpus provides no evidence of it.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Macnair's motivations are simple but consistent: he is loyal to Voldemort and to power, he enjoys killing, and he operates within Ministry structures as cover. His gratitude to Voldemort ('Thank you, Master') and Hagrid's description of him as a maniac who likes killing establish a motivation rooted in sadism and servility rather than ideology or internal conflict. There is no meaningful internal conflict — he is a flat villain defined by appetite and obedience.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: Even the minimal motivational texture present in the books — the sadism, the Voldemort-worship — cannot be confirmed as present in the films.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Absence of film material prevents any confirmation. Score reflects inability to verify even the simplest motivational preservation.

character_arc.book_baseline: Macnair has a flat arc that is intentionally so: he begins as a Ministry-employed executioner with Death Eater ties, returns to Voldemort's service, participates in key battles, and is defeated at the Battle of Hogwarts. There is no redemption, no growth, no change — he is a consistent instrument of violence. A flat arc faithfully preserved in the films would score full marks.
character_arc.difference: The flat arc cannot be confirmed as preserved or present.
character_arc.penalty_logic: Even a flat arc requires some presence to be confirmed as intact. With zero film evidence, no arc of any kind can be assessed.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Macnair's key relationships are: (1) Lucius Malfoy — described by Hagrid as 'an old pal', suggesting a pre-existing alliance that explains his Ministry position; (2) Voldemort — a master/servant relationship defined by Macnair's fawning gratitude and Voldemort's transactional promises; (3) Hagrid — an adversarial relationship across PoA (the execution) and OotP (the giant mission, where Macnair hunts Hagrid in the mountains). These relationships carry characterization weight.
key_relationships.difference: None of these relationships can be assessed as preserved.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: Zero film evidence means zero confirmation of any relational dynamic.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Macnair is not a complex character, but he has specific texture: the detail that he is Malfoy's 'old pal' placed inside the Ministry as executioner; Voldemort's specific address to him promising 'better victims'; Hagrid's characterisation of him as a maniac who enjoys killing; his active role hunting Hagrid and Olympe in the mountains; his combat role in the DoM; his defeat by Hagrid at the Battle of Hogwarts — a narratively satisfying callback to PoA. These details give him more texture than a generic Death Eater.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: No film material present. All of this texture is unrepresented in the corpus.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The Malfoy connection, the Voldemort graveyard scene address, the giant recruitment mission, the DoM combat, and the Hagrid callback at the Battle of Hogwarts are all absent from the film corpus.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The loss of the PoA execution scene tension, the GoF graveyard naming, the OotP DoM fight, and the DH Hagrid callback removes all the specific material that distinguishes Macnair from a generic background Death Eater. This is a near-total loss of what little complexity he had.

lost_or_transferred_material: Macnair's cold-voiced 'Where is the beast?' line and his presence as executioner creating plot tension in PoA
The precise narrative function of Macnair being called away to sign paperwork — the window that enables Buckbeak's rescue
Macnair's appearance at the graveyard in GoF, addressed personally by Voldemort with the promise of 'better victims'
Macnair's fawning 'Thank you, Master... thank you' — the only dialogue establishing his servile relationship with Voldemort
Macnair's role recruiting giants for Voldemort in OotP, and hunting Hagrid and Madame Maxime in the mountains
Macnair as active DoM combatant, assigned by Lucius to search with Avery, knocked out by Neville
Macnair thrown into a wall by Hagrid at the Battle of Hogwarts — a callback to PoA closing his narrative loop
The Malfoy/Macnair friendship establishing how Death Eaters infiltrated Ministry positions
score_caps_applied: Cap 4 (score above 70 not permitted when films preserve only broad identity but remove most defining book characterization): applied — Macnair is effectively absent from the film corpus entirely, making even this cap generous
Cap 3 (score above 75 not permitted when core book functions or acts of agency are transferred or removed): applied
comparative.key_observations: The film corpus for Macnair is entirely empty — no screenplay scenes are provided. This means his portrayal cannot be assessed as faithful in any dimension. The books establish him as a minor but textured villain with a consistent personality (cold, sadistic, servile), real plot agency across three books (PoA execution mechanism, GoF graveyard, OotP DoM combat, DH Battle of Hogwarts), a specific relationship network (Malfoy, Voldemort, Hagrid), and a narratively satisfying arc closure when Hagrid defeats him. All of this is unrepresented in the film corpus. The score of 15 reflects near-total absence rather than active distortion — the films may have included brief appearances not captured here, but based solely on the provided corpus, Macnair is functionally absent from the film adaptation. Confidence is Medium rather than Low because the book baseline is clear and consistent; the uncertainty is entirely on the film side.
